currentUse: Vacant former church complex (church, Madonna Hall, rectory) at 15-21 East 23rd Street, owned by St. John Paul II Church (RC successor parish), under an adopted city redevelopment plan and a 2025 site-plan application to demolish the entire complex for a six-story, 114-unit mixed-use building
summary: St. Michael's Lithuanian church complex at 15-21 East 23rd Street, Bayonne still stands but is vacant and slated for demolition. The parish was consolidated by the Archdiocese of Newark (announced 2015) and merged in 2016 with St. Joseph's and Our Lady of Assumption into the new St. John Paul II Parish. The building then served the Syriac Catholic community: St. Joseph Syriac Catholic Cathedral relocated to the East 23rd Street property (gcatholic dates the relocation to 2018) and used it until the eparchy's episcopal seat was moved to Farmington Hills, Michigan on July 1, 2022; city records from August 2024 list both lots as still owned by St. John Paul II Church, not the Syriac eparchy. Bayonne designated the site an area in need of redevelopment (council resolution of September 2023) and adopted the "15 East 23rd Street Redevelopment Plan" by ordinance O-24-56 on October 16, 2024, which describes the church as defunct and abandoned and requires the redeveloper to install a commemorative sign recognizing the church's significance to the community. In August 2025, 15 East 23rd St. Urban Renewal LLC, contract purchaser of the property, went before the Planning Board seeking approval to demolish the entire complex and build a six-story building with 114 residential units, 119 parking spaces, and about 2,700 sq ft of retail; a memorializing resolution for the application appeared on the board's September 9, 2025 agenda. No demolition had been reported in sources found through July 2026.

developments.headline: Parish consolidated into St. John Paul II Parish
developments.detail: The Archdiocese of Newark announced in 2015 that St. Michael's (21 East 23rd Street), St. Joseph's (Avenue E), and Our Lady of Assumption would close and merge; the merger creating St. John Paul II Parish took effect in 2016.

sources.title: Mount Carmel has been a fervent incubator of Polish culture, Catholic faith in Bayonne
St. Joseph Cathedral (Bayonne, New Jersey)

developments.headline: Building used as St. Joseph Syriac Catholic Cathedral
developments.detail: The Syriac Catholic cathedral parish of St. Joseph (established 2010, cathedral from 2011) relocated in 2018 from Avenue E to 21 E. 23rd St., the former St. Michael's property; TrueLithuania describes a partial transfer with time-sharing and the Lithuanian sign eventually covered, while Lithuanian-inscribed stations of the cross remained inside. Cathedral status ended when Pope Francis approved moving the eparchy's seat to St. Toma in Farmington Hills, Michigan on July 1, 2022.

developments.headline: Site designated for redevelopment study
developments.detail: Bayonne Municipal Council Resolution No. 23-09-20-061 (dated September 13, 2023, cited in ordinance O-24-56) set in motion the area-in-need-of-redevelopment process for Block 198, Lots 1 and 2 (15-21 East 23rd Street).

sources.title: Ordinance O-24-56

developments.headline: Redevelopment plan adopted; owner listed as St. John Paul II Church
developments.detail: Ordinance O-24-56 adopted the '15 East 23rd Street Redevelopment Plan' (dated August 2, 2024). The plan describes the site as a defunct, abandoned church with Madonna Hall (built as the parish hall) and a rectory, lists both lots as owned by St. John Paul II Church (property class 15D, Religious and Charitable), permits mixed-use redevelopment, and requires the redeveloper to install a St. Michael's Church commemorative sign of 8-40 square feet.

sources.title: 15 East 23rd Street Redevelopment Plan
sources.publisher: City of Bayonne Division of Planning and Zoning

sources.title: Ordinance O-24-56

developments.headline: Planning Board hears 114-unit demolition/redevelopment application
developments.detail: 15 East 23rd St. Urban Renewal LLC, contract purchaser of the property (application P-25-016, developer Skye Development per Jersey Digs), sought preliminary and final major site plan approval to demolish the entire complex and build a six-story mixed-use building: 114 residential units, 119 parking spaces, ~2,701 sq ft of retail, gym, roof deck. Jersey Digs reported the church had been vacant for several years and that Bayonne's Historical Committee offered no objection to demolition.

sources.title: Bayonne to Consider 114-Unit Development at Former St. Michael's Site
Statement of the Applicant, 15 East 23rd St. Urban Renewal LLC (P-25-016)

developments.headline: Memorializing resolution for the application on Planning Board agenda
developments.detail: The Bayonne Planning Board's September 9, 2025 regular meeting agenda listed adoption of a resolution for '15 EAST 23RD ST., LLC - Site: 15 East 23rd Street; Block 198, Lots 1 and 2' under Adoption of Resolutions, indicating the board acted on the application at its prior hearing; the vote outcome itself was not found in accessible sources.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: The archive note "Sold; became a Syrian/Assyrian Catholic cathedral" needs two corrections and a major update. (1) Rite: the occupant was the Syriac Catholic Church (St. Joseph Cathedral of the Eparchy of Our Lady of Deliverance), not Assyrian. (2) "Sold" is contradicted for the cathedral transaction: TrueLithuania describes a formal partial transfer with time-sharing, and Bayonne's August 2024 redevelopment plan lists both lots as still owned by St. John Paul II Church — the Roman Catholic successor parish — so no verified sale to the Syriac church occurred; the sale actually in progress is to a developer (15 East 23rd St. Urban Renewal LLC, "contract purchaser" as of its 2025 application). (3) The record is stale: the cathedral era ended July 1, 2022 when the eparchy's seat moved to Michigan, the complex is vacant and city-designated as defunct/abandoned, and as of August-September 2025 a demolition-and-114-unit-redevelopment application had gone through the Planning Board. The "closed" status and "interior retained Lithuanian details" note (Lithuanian-inscribed stations of the cross per TrueLithuania) are consistent with findings.
gaps: Parish founding date not verified (TrueLithuania dates only the current towerless building to 1977; the parish is presumably much older but no source found stating it). No sale price or closing date found for the transfer to the developer LLC — as of the 2025 application it was still "contract purchaser," and county deed records were not searched. The exact outcome/vote of the August 12, 2025 Planning Board hearing was not directly confirmed; the memorializing resolution on the September 9, 2025 agenda strongly implies the board acted, but the resolution text was not found. No news of actual demolition found through July 2026, so "standing" reflects the latest available sources (August-September 2025), not a confirmed 2026 observation. The current disposition of the Lithuanian interior elements (stations of the cross with Lithuanian inscriptions) is unknown. A TAPinto Bayonne article on the redevelopment ("Is St. Michael's Church the Next One Facing Redevelopment?") returned HTTP 403 and could not be read; the city's area-in-need-of-redevelopment study PDF (P-23-024) is a scanned image file whose text could not be extracted. No evidence found of preservation efforts, parishioner reunions, or Lithuanian community activity around the closure or redevelopment.
